name: The Tyranny of Numbers: Counting Calories, Counting Growth
description:
What constitutes "good statistics"?

This lecture explores how some of the most familiar indicators: calories often used to depict the quality of our diet and Gross Domestic Product, which does the same for the economy, illustrate both the power and the limits of measurement. Calories provide a useful estimate of energy intake, but they do not fully capture nutritional quality, health outcomes, or the lived experience of eating. In a similar way, GDP measures economic output, but not inequality, environmental stress, unpaid care work, or the broader quality of life. In both cases, a single statistic can illuminate one dimension of reality while obscuring others.

Through this discussion TCA Anant examines why modern institutions are so drawn to numerical indicators. Governments need targets, firms need metrics, and citizens seek simple benchmarks in an increasingly complex world. But when measures become goals in themselves, distortions often follow: food engineered for labels rather than nourishment, education reduced to test scores, or growth pursued without regard to employment, sustainability, or social well-being.

name: Mise en Play: Play as a Creative Tool
description:
Every day, without thinking about it, you make sense of your surroundings through what you eat, what you hear, what your body tells you. We are all meaning-making creatures, and our earliest instinct was to do this through play. Play is a creative act in itself. Growing up, play was how we explored, expressed, and understood the world. Somewhere along the way, we stopped. Here is an invitation to start again.

In this edition of Mise En Play, we use the same lens to explore different exhibits as a part of CALORIE. A space where many different perspectives and processes come together, and in doing so, we begin to see creativity not as a skill but as a practice. That practice is what we invite you into.

Expect hands-on activities that engage all your senses. You need no background in art or design, only an openness to notice, engage, and explore. Leave with a new way of observing your environment and documenting your experiences.

Join experimentors Nirupama and Shreya for a programme that treats creativity as something that belongs to everyone.

name: Thinking on Food at the Margins
description: Food is not just about sustenance and nourishment in India. It is deeply influenced by the social, cultural and political landscape. In Dalit literature, descriptions of food become metaphors for cultural and social hierarchy, and it emerges as a powerful narrative that archives stories of struggle and resilience. In this programme, Rajyashri Goody, Artist; and Shahu Patole, Author of Dalit Kitchens of Marathwada; reflect on the politics of food and representation in India as seen in literature. The conversation will be moderated by Shambhawi Vikram.

name: Anna CALORIE-ina - The Literary in CALORIE
description:
What are some things that remind you of the stories you have read? Are they coffee bars across the city named Malgudi, after Swami and Friends’ infamous fictional village? Or could it even be an exhibition?

CALORIE, an exhibition exploring food systems, is replete with stories of people, movements, strife and struggles, like so many books. Come engage with these stories, both in the exhibits, as well as in some classics, some unfamiliar, and some very unique novels and short stories. 

Join Megha, an experimentor, to explore Anna CALORIE-ina - The Literary in CALORIE.

name: Rewriting the Recipe
description:
What can recipes teach us? 

The recipe, as a form, encodes more than method. In the South Asian context, it archives labour, caste hierarchy, memory, and resistance. Food knowledge within Dalit communities has rarely been formally archived. Unwritten, unseen, unacknowledged by the other, cut out not through spectacular erasure but through the quiet violence of being considered inconsequential. 

This workshop asks how one can play with text to surface what a simple recipe may not be able to hold. Participants bring their own or others' writing about food, which is read aloud, exchanged, and subjected to processes of editing, collage, and recomposition. Through collaborative writing and textual appropriation, the workshop explores the new meanings that emerge when texts are redistributed and rewritten in different hands.

name: Kneading CALORIE: Bodily Movements in Food
description:
Ever wonder how food moves before it reaches our tables? 

Farmers sweep sickles through stalks of grain, cutting and gathering the harvest. It travels to markets, then into kitchens where hands chop, fold, and knead ingredients, before finally reaching tables where people gather to share meals. Food fuels our bodies, helping us walk, think, and work. Food and movement are inseparable parts of everyday life.

Join Beverly through Kneading Calories, a walkthrough where she explores the coordination and human effort behind food.

name: Malleshwaram Bakery Walk
description:
What can a bakery reveal about a city?

Join us for a guided walk through some of Bengaluru’s neighbourhood bakeries as we trace the histories, communities, and everyday food cultures that shaped them. Beginning in the heart of Malleshwaram, the walk moves through bakeries known for warm khara buns, sweet dilpasands, plain bread cake and more.

name: Second Serving: Food and Memory
description:
Second Serving is a two-day public film screening series, born out of our CALORIE film festival season. Day 1 screens a curated selection of short animated films - hand-drawn, stop-motion, and experimental - all circling food in some way. Films that look at food as memory, as labour, as metaphor. Some tender, some strange, some both.

This will be followed by some activities and refreshments.

name: Nature and Nurture: How The Environment Shapes Our Genes
description:
How does our environment and genetics affect our health?

The nature versus nurture debate has evolved into a more integrated view, recognising that genes and environment work together throughout our lives. While our genetic makeup – nature – provides the blueprint for potential traits, the environment – nurture – shapes how these genes are expressed, often through ‘epigenetic’ mechanisms. 

Join Sanjeev Galande as he explores how understanding human behaviour, health, and development requires considering both genetic and environmental factors. Particularly, highlighting the technological breakthroughs and transdisciplinary approaches that have deepened our understanding of disease susceptibility, with a focus on lifestyle disorders.

name: Reading the Garden
description:
What knowledge, experience, and histories does a garden hold?

Gardens are often understood as places of beauty, leisure, and recreation. But they are also archives of memories that have travelled with people across time and culture. Every plant carries within it stories of movement, exchange, and survival: journeys that span continents, centuries, and knowledge systems. Over time, many become so familiar that we forget they were once newcomers, or that the ways we know them are themselves a kind of history.

Consider the tamarind. A botanist attends to the leaf structure. A storyteller encodes a myth in the same form. An Ayurvedic physician describes what it does to the body. A grandmother knows exactly how much pulp changes the taste of a dish. None of these is the definitive account. Each is answering a different question about the same plant, and each reveals as much about the culture as about the plant itself.

​Taking Lalbagh Botanical Garden as its point of departure, this workshop invites participants to approach plants as objects that carry multiple and sometimes contradictory histories: botanical and mythological, medicinal and culinary, linguistic and deeply personal.

name: Gulistan (The Land of Flowers) and Other Histories
description:
How do plants travel across cultures, histories, and oceans?

Join Gopa Trivedi, artist of Gulistan, which maps a garden of ingredients that travelled far before landing on Indian soil—seeds carried by trade, colonial routes, and ocean currents. In this talk, she shares the explorations, thoughts, questions, and journeys that shape and inform her visual arts practice: through images, ideas, and encounters that have accumulated over a decade of working at the intersection of the personal, the historical, and the deeply uncertain.
